name: Diophantine Equations — Whole-Number Solutions
whatIsIt: A DIOPHANTINE equation asks for INTEGER (whole-number) solutions only. The classic linear one is ax + by = c. The key rule: it has integer solutions IF AND ONLY IF gcd(a, b) divides c. When one solution exists, there are INFINITELY many — you shift x by b/g and y by −a/g (where g = gcd). These count-only problems (coins, tickets, buses) are pure Olympiad number theory.
whyNeeded: Real problems often need whole answers — you can't buy 2.5 tickets. Diophantine thinking tells you instantly whether a whole-number combination is even possible, and how to list them all.
realLifeUses: Making an exact amount with only ₹2 and ₹5 coins
Buying tickets of two prices to spend an exact total
Fitting boxes of two sizes to fill a shelf exactly
Scheduling events that must line up on whole days

vocabulary.meaning: An equation where only integer solutions are allowed.
ax + by = c has integer solutions ⟺ gcd(a, b) divides c.
If one solution exists, infinitely many do (shift by b/g and a/g).

story.title: Exact Change
story.text: Priya wants to pay exactly ₹5 using only ₹3 and ₹6 coins: 3x + 6y = 5. Uncle checks: 'gcd(3, 6) = 3, and 3 does NOT divide 5 — impossible!' Then she tries ₹12 with ₹2 and ₹3 coins: 2x + 3y = 12. 'gcd(2, 3) = 1, which divides 12 — solvable! x = 0, y = 4 works, or x = 3, y = 2.' Priya grins: 'The GCD decides if exact change is even possible.'
story.extractedProblem: Can 3x + 6y = 5 be solved in whole numbers?
story.answerInStory: No — gcd(3, 6) = 3 does not divide 5, so there are no integer solutions.

visual.caption: A linear Diophantine equation is solvable in whole numbers exactly when gcd(a, b) divides c — then solutions come in an evenly-spaced family.

standardMethod.summary: Compute g = gcd(a, b). If g does not divide c, there are NO integer solutions. If it does, find one solution (by inspection or the extended Euclidean algorithm); all others come by shifting x by b/g and y by −a/g.
steps: If g does not divide c → no integer solutions; stop.
If g divides c, find one solution (try small values, or use Bézout).
Generate the whole family: x + (b/g)t, y − (a/g)t for integer t.
So there are NO integer solutions.

commonMistakes.mistake: Trying to solve when gcd(a, b) does not divide c.
commonMistakes.fix: First check: integer solutions exist ONLY IF gcd(a, b) divides c. If not, stop — there are none.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking there is just one solution.
commonMistakes.fix: If one integer solution exists, there are INFINITELY many — shift x by b/g and y by −a/g.

easy.q: ax + by = c has integer solutions if and only if gcd(a, b)…

questions.q: ax + by = c is solvable in integers iff…
If one integer solution exists, how many are there?

teachBackPrompt: With coins of two values (say ₹3 and ₹6), try to make ₹5 exactly, then ₹12 with ₹2 and ₹3 coins. Explain to a grown-up how the GCD tells you instantly whether the exact amount is even possible.

revisionCard.summary: Diophantine = integer solutions only. ax + by = c is solvable ⟺ gcd(a, b) divides c. If so, there are infinitely many (shift x by b/g, y by −a/g). Always check the GCD condition first.

rememberIt.hook: ax + by = c solvable ⟺ gcd(a,b) | c; then infinitely many
rememberIt.unpack: No GCD divide, no solution. One solution ⇒ a whole shifting family.

studentQuestions.q: How do I list all the solutions?
studentQuestions.a: Find one (x₀, y₀), then every solution is x₀ + (b/g)t, y₀ − (a/g)t for integer t, where g = gcd(a, b).
The left side ax + by is always a multiple of gcd(a, b), so c must be too — otherwise no whole numbers can ever hit c.

formulas.name: Solvability
formulas.formula: ax + by = c solvable ⟺ gcd(a, b) | c
